draft.introduction: The Enchiridion (Handbook) opens with the fundamental Stoic distinction between what is “up to us” (eph’ hemin) and what is not. This division between the internal realm of judgment and the external realm of circumstance is the foundation of Stoic practice.

translation.text: Of things, some are up to us and some are not. Up to us are judgment, impulse, desire, and aversion—in a word, whatever is our own doing. Not up to us are the body, possessions, reputation, and office—in a word, whatever is not our own doing. The things up to us are by nature free, unhindered, and unimpeded; the things not up to us are weak, slavish, hindered, and belong to others.
translation.original_text: τῶν ὄντων τὰ μέν ἐστιν ἐφ᾽ ἡμῖν, τὰ δὲ οὐκ ἐφ᾽ ἡμῖν

translator_notes.note: The distinction between ἐφ᾽ ἡμῖν (up to us) and οὐκ ἐφ᾽ ἡμῖν (not up to us) became the most influential practical teaching in the Stoic tradition. The Serenity Prayer of modern recovery movements echoes this division directly.
